item.title: Visual novel and Roger has topped 100,000 sales worldwide
item.content:encoded:
Together, we power an unparalleled network of 220+ online properties covering 10,000+ granular topics, serving an audience of 50+ million professionals with original, objective content from trusted sources. We help you gain critical insights and make more informed decisions across your business priorities.

This site is operated by a business or businesses owned by Informa PLC and all copyright resides with them. Informa PLC's registered office is 5 Howick Place, London SW1P 1WG. Registered in England and Wales. Number 8860726.

  • Business
  • Production
  • News

Solo developer TearyHand Studio said players will forever have their 'eternal gratitude.'

Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 17, 2026

1 Min Read

Image via TearyHand Studio

Newsbrief: Point-and-click visual novel and Roger has sold 100,000 copies in around eight months.

The indie title, developed by TearyHand Studio and published by Kodansha, was last week honoured with the Audience Award at the 2026 Game Developers Choice Awards. It has also received a BAFTA nomination in the 'Game Beyond Entertainment' category. 

Since launching in July 2025, and Roger has been recognized with 13 awards and nominations, including including the Best Arts, Best Presentation, and Grand Prix awards at Tokyo Game Show.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 

Daily news, dev blogs, and stories from Game Developer straight to your inbox



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/visual-novel-and-roger-has-topped-100-000-sales-worldwide

item.title: Raw Fury boss Pim Holfve is the new CEO of Combined Effect
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 17, 2026

1 Min Read

Logo via Combined Effect / Blue Prince artwork via Raw Fury

Raw Fury parent company Combined Effect has appointed Pim Holfve as its new CEO. That's a fairly notable move when you consider Holfve is also the CEO of Raw Fury.

The promotion was described as an expansion of Holfve's leadership remit in a press release, which confirmed he will continue to oversee Raw Fury. 

The indie publisher saw revenue grow by 43 percent in 2025 under Holfve. That upswing was attributed to the successful release of titles such as Blue Prince, ROUTINE and The Seance of Blake Manor.

Holfve said he intends to transform Combined Effect—which also owns Fury Studios and Kakehashi Games—into an "enabler and catalyst" for indie success. 

"Combined Effect will act as an enabler and catalyst, where publishers and studios within the group can share and learn from each other, supercharging the support they can offer and providing a strong platform for bold developers and original games," said Holfve. "Essentially, we offer the best of both worlds, where publishers can grow autonomously but also benefit from shared expertise, industry know-how and a strategic vision for success."

Related:Krafton to invest $1 billion into South Korean defense company partnered with the U.S. navy

Mattias Miksche, chairman of the board at Combined Effect, said Holfve demonstrated "exceptional leadership" when guiding Raw Fury through its most successful year in history. "I'm confident that under his direction, the group will continue to champion both artistic and commercially successful titles," he added.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/raw-fury-boss-pim-holfve-is-the-new-ceo-of-combined-effect

item.title: Krafton to invest $1 billion into South Korean defense company partnered with the U.S. navy
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 17, 2026

2 Min Read

Subnautica and PUBG maker Krafton has agreed to sink around $1 billion into Hanwha Aerospace's global AI, robotics, and defense initiatives. 

The company has penned a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the South Korean multinational as part of a broader effort to invest in physical AI. 

As noted in an announcement on the Krafton website, the strategic alliance will see the two companies work in tandem to "develop and commercialize technology across various sectors, including defense." 

It will also result in Krafton investing into fund established by Hanwha Asset Management "centered on AI, robotics, and defense," with a target size of $1 billion.

Hanwha finances and develops a number of defense systems across land, air, sea, cyber, and space—including the K9 self-propelled howitzer, which it notes includes "precision-guided munitions and multiple rocket launchers."

In 2024, Hanwha became the first in Korea to secure and carry out maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) projects for the U.S. Navy after agreeing to service logistics support vessels including the USNS Wally Schirra. 

Related:Krafton loses lawsuit, is ordered to reinstate ousted Unknown Worlds CEO

Krafton said it will leverage its experience in "operating large-scale game data and physics-based virtual worlds serve" as core assets when training and verifying physical AI software, and explained it also intends to launch a joint venture with Hanwha to "seamlessly advance joint development outcomes from implementation to commercialization." 

Notably, the South Korean publisher claimed that Hanwha has the potential to establish itself as a leading global defence technology company, before comparing it to Anduril—the U.S. military contractor established by Oculus founder Palmer Luckey.

Hanwha Aerospace President and CEO Jae-il Son said the company's partnership with Krafton will deliver a "new paradigm standard in areas of physical AI and future defense."

Krafton announced its partnership with Hanwha on March 13, just days before losing its high-profile legal dispute with the co-founders of Subnautica developer Unknown Worlds.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/krafton-to-invest-1-billion-into-south-korea-defense-company-partnered-with-the-u-s-navy
